name: Attendee 1
headline: Founder & CEO at ExampleHealth — building voice agents for clinical workflows
recent_posts: Spent the weekend debugging latency in our STT pipeline — turns out the keyword boost list was the bottleneck, not the model.
Hot take: most 'AI scribes' are just thin wrappers on Whisper. The differentiation is in how you handle clinician interrupts mid-utterance.
We just shipped a Toast POS integration in two weeks. Half the work was reverse-engineering their auth flow.
one_liner: Currently obsessed with sub-500ms voice agent latency for clinical scribing; just shipped a Toast POS integration last week.

name: Attendee 2
headline: Partner at ExampleVentures — leads seed and Series A in dev tools, AI infra, and vertical SaaS
recent_posts: Wrote a memo on why AI infra spend is going to consolidate around 3 model providers by EOY 2027. Happy to share with founders.
Met with 8 dev tools founders this week. The good ones all have the same answer to 'what's your moat': distribution, not the model.
Bullish on voice agents replacing 1-800 IVRs. The vertical-first players (med, dental, restaurant) are pulling ahead of horizontal.
one_liner: Actively writing $1.5–4M seed checks into vertical AI; especially loves voice agent companies with real revenue and a wedge into a specific industry.

name: Attendee 3
headline: Senior Staff Engineer at ExampleCorp — distributed systems, infra, formerly at Stripe
recent_posts: Postgres logical replication is criminally underused for CDC. We replaced our Kafka pipeline with it and cut infra costs 80%.
If your service has p99 latency >2s and you haven't profiled the GC, you're leaving free wins on the table.
Wrote up our migration off Kubernetes onto Fly machines — 3x faster deploys, 1/4 the YAML.
one_liner: Deep infra expertise, just left Stripe; quietly looking for a small early-stage team to join as employee #1–5 if the founders are technical.

name: Attendee 4
headline: Product Designer at ExampleStudio — focused on agentic UX and tool-use interfaces
recent_posts: Designing for agents is a different beast: you're not designing screens, you're designing the moments where the agent asks for human input.
Spent the day prototyping a chat UI where the agent's tool calls are visible inline. Users trust it 4x more than a black box.
Hot take: 90% of 'AI products' have terrible loading states. If your model takes 8s, you should design for 8s, not pretend it's 1s.
one_liner: Cares deeply about how agents communicate uncertainty and tool use; will absolutely tear apart any AI product that hides what the model is doing.

name: Attendee 5
headline: Co-founder at ExamplePay — fintech infra for SMB lending, ex-Plaid
recent_posts: Underwriting SMBs is impossible without bank data and impossible WITH bank data unless you can categorize transactions in real time. We built our own.
The Plaid playbook (free dev tier, paid usage tier) doesn't work in fintech infra anymore. Compliance kills the free tier economics.
Spent the weekend scraping IRS Form 941 data for 50k SMBs to train a default-risk model. The data is open; people just don't know.
one_liner: Looking for a technical co-founder with ML background to lead underwriting models; just raised a $4M seed from sector specialists.

name: Attendee 6
headline: ML Research Engineer at ExampleLabs — RAG, retrieval, embedding models
recent_posts: Reranking with a cross-encoder beats hybrid search 9 times out of 10 if your corpus is under 100k docs. Stop overengineering.
Cohere's rerank-3 vs BGE-reranker-v2: ran a head-to-head on 12 internal benchmarks. Cohere wins on noisy queries, BGE wins on long docs.
If your RAG system has >20% irrelevant retrievals, you don't have a model problem, you have a chunking problem.
one_liner: Has opinions about every embedding model on Hugging Face; recently became obsessed with rerankers and is writing a benchmark suite.

name: Attendee 7
headline: Founder at ExampleEats — voice ordering for independent restaurants
recent_posts: Watched 40 hours of restaurant phone calls last week. The #1 reason orders fail isn't the AI mishearing — it's the menu being structured wrong.
We just hit 1000 calls/day across 12 pilot restaurants. Conversion went from 28% to 41% after we cut our greeting from 28 words to 17.
If you're building voice agents and you haven't transcribed your own product's calls and read them, you don't have a product, you have a demo.
one_liner: Just hit 41% order conversion across 12 pilot restaurants by ruthlessly shortening prompts; will trade notes on VAPI configs all night.

name: Attendee 8
headline: Indie hacker — building Mythcraft (procedural-narrative game) and selling Notion templates on the side
recent_posts: Hit $4k MRR on the Notion template store this month. The killer template was 'AI Engineer Job Tracker' — built it in 3 hours.
Procedural narrative is hard because LLMs are good at sentences but bad at long-arc consequence. Spent the weekend on a memory layer that fixes this.
Drank way too much oat milk matcha at Stonemill yesterday. Anyone know a place in SF that does it without the maple syrup?
one_liner: Solo dev grossing $4k MRR on Notion templates while building a procedural-narrative game; will absolutely talk shop about matcha and SF cafés.

name: Attendee 9
headline: Growth Lead at ExampleSaaS — B2B SaaS, PLG, ex-Notion
recent_posts: PLG is dead at the enterprise tier. Every PLG company over $50M ARR has quietly bolted on a sales-led motion. Stop pretending.
Best onboarding I've seen this year: Linear's. Worst: literally every AI product I've tried. The bar is in the floor.
Hot take: 'product-led growth' is just 'good product' rebranded. If your product needs a growth team to grow, it's not actually PLG.
one_liner: Has run PLG at scale and now thinks it's overrated past Series B; really wants to talk to founders about hybrid sales + PLG motions.

name: Attendee 10
headline: Hardware engineer at ExampleRobotics — surgical robotics, formerly at Intuitive
recent_posts: Surgical robotics is 90% mechanical engineering and 10% software, but the 10% software is what determines whether the FDA clears you.
Spent the weekend at a boba spot in Mountain View grinding through a kinematics paper. 9 hours, 4 boba teas. Recommend Tea Top.
If you're building hardware and you haven't done a teardown of your closest competitor, you don't deserve to ship.
one_liner: 20 years in surgical robotics, now exploring AI-augmented surgical planning; weekends are 50% kinematics papers and 50% boba taxonomy.
